competitors.label: Angular Outputs Guide
competitors.selectionReason: Angular.dev splits inputs and outputs into separate official guides; the outputs guide is the stronger canonical competitor because it covers event semantics, naming, and non-bubbling behavior.
competitors.winReason: The official guide is authoritative for outputs, but our page is stronger on the combined parent-child contract and on the senior pitfalls around EventEmitter misuse, input timing, and custom two-way binding.
theirStrengths: It is the authoritative reference for custom events, emit semantics, naming, and the fact that Angular outputs do not bubble.
theirGaps: It is centered on outputs and does not combine parent-to-child inputs, change-over-time behavior, and child-to-parent events into one decision model.

realWorldUseCases: Interviewers expect you to explain timing (updates over time), typing, OnPush/immutability implications, and when NOT to use EventEmitter (cross-component event bus).
followUpConfusion: The correct answers are: ngOnChanges , an @Input setter, or (in newer Angular) signals-based inputs.
This is a common interview topic (and a common place to make naming mistakes).

realWorldUseCases: The outputs guide is authoritative on event semantics, but it is not as centered on the full parent-child API contract and its production misuse patterns.
actionableExamples: Angular.dev has concrete emit and alias examples, so the example comparison stays close.
followUpConfusion: The docs explain non-bubbling, aliases, and naming, but they do not answer as much about input updates over time or when EventEmitter becomes the wrong abstraction.

actionableExamples: Add one explicit before-and-after example where a child incorrectly mutates parent state and then switches to emit intent up.

competitors.label: GreatFrontEnd Angular Basic Interview Questions
competitors.selectionReason: This guide is GreatFrontEnd’s main Angular fundamentals article and a fair interview-prep baseline for component communication concepts.
competitors.winReason: Our page is more explicit about data down / intent up, how inputs evolve after first render, and why EventEmitter is not a cross-component event bus.
theirStrengths: It covers Angular fundamentals in a concise interview-prep tone.
theirGaps: It is lighter on the pitfalls seniors usually mention around input timing, custom two-way binding, and event semantics.

realWorldUseCases: @Input() is the child component’s public data API .
@Output() is the child component’s public event API .
followUpConfusion: The correct answers are: ngOnChanges , an @Input setter, or (in newer Angular) signals-based inputs.
This is a common interview topic (and a common place to make naming mistakes).

realWorldUseCases: GreatFrontEnd stays at the broader Angular-fundamentals level, while our page is directly about parent-child data contracts and event boundaries.
actionableExamples: Its examples are concise enough that the example criterion stays close.
followUpConfusion: The guide is lighter on input-change timing and on the EventEmitter misuse follow-up that often appears in senior interviews.

actionableExamples: Add one tiny custom two-way binding example that shows the exact x and xChange contract side by side.

competitors.label: Angular University @Output Guide
competitors.selectionReason: This tutorial is a strong direct competitor because it goes deep on EventEmitter semantics, event naming, and the command-versus-event pitfall.
theirStrengths: It goes deep on event design mistakes, naming, and what an output should represent.
theirGaps: It is output-centric and less balanced across the full input/output contract than our page.

realWorldUseCases: Integrated example: input immutability + typed output event
actionableExamples: renameRequested.emit({ id: this.user.id, nextName: this.draftName || this.user.name });

realWorldUseCases: Angular University also frames outputs through a real design pitfall, so the practical use-case comparison stays close.
actionableExamples: It offers more worked output examples and more detailed event-design guidance than our compact trivia.
followUpConfusion: It answers command-versus-event confusion well, so the follow-up comparison remains close.

realWorldUseCases: Add one example that combines input immutability with output event design in a single parent-child component pair.
actionableExamples: Add a second worked example showing a typed payload object and how the parent consumes $event.
followUpConfusion: Keep one extra note on how wrapped native elements do not automatically bubble component outputs upward.

competitors.winReason: Our page now combines input immutability, typed output payloads, and non-bubbling event behavior in one parent-child example, making it stronger on use-cases and examples while follow-up depth stays close.
